critique.summary: The article describes a detailed technical process of decoding UDP packets starting from raw voltage signals captured by an oscilloscope on a QSGMII Ethernet link. The author explains the steps from physical layer signal capture through 8b/10b decoding, stream separation, and packet reconstruction, culminating in generating pcap files for analysis with Wireshark.
mainPoints: UDP operates at the transport layer (Layer 4) of the OSI model and is used for sending messages over IP networks.
The author captures raw waveform data from a QSGMII link between a VSC7448 switch and a VSC8504 PHY using a high-speed differential probe and an oscilloscope.
QSGMII combines four SGMII channels into one high-speed serial link using 8b/10b encoding, requiring decoding of code-groups and synchronization using comma characters.
The decoded data stream is demultiplexed into four separate streams corresponding to the four ports by detecting special code-groups (K28.1 and K28.5).
The streams are further decoded into Ethernet frames, revealing UDP packets which are then exported into pcap files for analysis with standard tools like Wireshark and tshark.
The entire decoding pipeline from raw waveform file to pcap files runs efficiently on the author's computer.
critique.truthfulness: The technical claims made in the article align with established networking standards and practices. The description of UDP as a Layer 4 protocol, the use of QSGMII and SGMII interfaces, 8b/10b encoding, and the role of comma characters for synchronization are consistent with IEEE and industry documentation. The author references IEEE 802.3-2015 and uses known tools like Wireshark and tshark to validate the decoded packets. The detailed code snippets and explanations correspond to real protocols and decoding techniques. The article is a credible, accurate technical walkthrough based on the author's direct experience and standard references.
considerations: The article assumes a high level of technical knowledge in networking and signal processing, which may limit accessibility to general audiences.
It focuses on a specific hardware setup (Oxide Computer Company's rack switch and PHY) and a particular use case, so some details may not generalize to all QSGMII or UDP scenarios.
The decoding approach is custom and home-grown; alternative commercial or open-source tools might offer different workflows or optimizations.
The article does not discuss error handling or robustness of the decoding process in noisy or less ideal signal conditions.
Security implications of decoding raw signals and analyzing network traffic are not addressed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the practical use of oscilloscopes and related tools for signal decoding and debugging, with participants sharing personal experiences, tool recommendations, and clarifications about technical details such as sampling rates.

comments.author: blindseeker
comments.text:
University professor here: showing and decoding wire signals has become a staple in my teaching - very effective, eye-opening even for students that find the OSI layers too abstract to grasp on paper. Btw, I’m using this nice demo board (I’m not affiliated with the brand): https://www.batronix.com/shop/demoboards/Batronix-MSO-Demo-B...

…typically we decode I2C.

Oh, I‘ve equipped the lab with all kinds of oscilloscopes. I find a lower mid-tier product (like Rigol DHO 900/1000 series) most suitable: good enough resolution and nice quality-of-life features (many physical buttons / dials and input channels go a long way!), yet not too feature-packed and physically compact to still be approachable.
comments.analysis: The comment provides credible, detailed insight from an educator's perspective, including specific equipment recommendations, which adds practical value and is consistent with common teaching practices.

comments.author: mjbright
comments.text:
This reminds me of back in the day (1985) on my final year project, I'd designed a (6502 or 8080 based) SBC, my PCB design, my assembler and on first boot ... nothing happened.

With only a voltmeter in hand, I measured the voltages on the address lines - thankfully my dumb programming error was a very tight loop so it was easy to deduce where the looping was happening !

Some things were easier back in the day.
comments.analysis: This personal anecdote is plausible and adds historical context about debugging with limited tools, reflecting the evolution of hardware development and troubleshooting.

comments.author: jzer0cool
comments.text: Could anyone recommend a budget tooling (has most features desired, please explain) to capture on wire like an oscilloscope. Help understand what kind of sample rates needed etc. features desired, cost and any recommended brand/models to get by.  Thx!
comments.analysis: A straightforward request for advice on affordable equipment; while not providing information itself, it reflects a genuine user need and encourages helpful responses.

comments.author: Retr0id
comments.text:
> I configured the oscilloscope to collect 100M samples at 1 TSPS

Typo? I didn't think we had sample rates anywhere near that high!
comments.analysis: This comment correctly questions an unusually high sampling rate claim, demonstrating technical knowledge and skepticism appropriate for verifying technical accuracy.

comments.author: Cadwhisker
comments.text:
I love the photo of the probe, and I bet it's very expensive. I wonder if the "A071626" sticker changes the impedance of the traces enough for you to notice :)

https://www.mattkeeter.com/blog/2022-08-11-udp/probes_full.j...
comments.analysis: This comment is more playful and speculative than informative, lightly mocking the equipment without contributing substantive technical insight.
